findings.description: Off-by-one: condition `len(hits) <= self.limit` allows the (limit+1)-th request through; should be `< self.limit`. On the boundary request, `remaining` also becomes -1.

`remaining` is computed after the hit is appended (`self.limit - len(hits)`), so it reports one less than the true remaining capacity; on the off-by-one boundary request it returns -1, a nonsensical value.

No thread-safety: the prune → check-length → append sequence in `check` is not atomic; concurrent callers for the same client key can both pass the guard simultaneously, allowing more requests than the limit.

`current_usage` accesses `self._hits[key]` on a defaultdict, silently creating an empty-list entry for every previously-unseen client; this is a hidden write side-effect in a read-only query method and pollutes the key space iterated by `sweep`.
`retry_after` can be zero or negative if the system clock is stepped backward (NTP slew or VM migration), returning a meaningless wait value to the caller.
Client key derived from `X-Forwarded-For` leftmost element, which any client can spoof; correct safe practice is the rightmost IP inserted by the trusted proxy. (Deferring detail to critic-security.)

`hits.pop(0)` on a plain list is O(n) per call, making the pruning loop O(n^2) in the worst case under burst traffic; a deque with popleft() is the correct structure. (Deferring detail to critic-performance.)

`sweep` only removes keys whose entire timestamp list is stale but never prunes the individual timestamps within kept buckets; a silent burst client's full timestamp list persists in memory until the window expires, contrary to the docstring claim of bounding memory.
